headlines.title: Judge Hunter Nails Some Campaign Misrepresentations
headlines.fullText:
Comanche, Texas, May 27th, 1914.
To the Public :
Mr. Oscar Callaway, in his speeches at Weatherford and at Arlington, and, I suppose, at Thorpe Springs and other places, made the emphatic statement that I am "drawing a pension of $24.00 per month," and I have heard that Mayor Milam is whispering the same around quietly in his gum-shoe campaign against me in our race for Congress. I don't consider that drawing a pension from the state of nation would be any disgrace or serions objection to any candidate for Congress. I rather consider it a mark of honor. 1, however, denounce the statement as absolutely false. A certificate from the Commissioner of Pensions at Washington would settle the question, and if they will produce such I will quit the race. I hope they will be gentlemen enough to withdraw such statements as publicly as they have made them, or at once produce the certificate of the Pension Commissioner.
I can prove every statement I have printed in my reasons for opposing the re-election of Mr. Callaway and more, and will do so. Mr. Bryan's certificate only states that "his attention had never been called to anything in the record attributed to you (Callaway ) * * * but if you (Callaway) ever said anything unfriendly of me (Bryan) I do not know it." Of course not, nor did the lion know that the gnat was stinging him on his mane. Mr. Callaway spoke his sarcastic, invective speech against Mr. Bryan during Tait's administration, and among his Republican friends and allies, with whom he stood and voted, and who applauded him vociferously when he made his little speech against the greatest man in the world. These great Republican leaders applauded his treachery to the Democratic leader of our progressive party, but no doubt, despised the MILAM. traitor.
SCORES
Ever since April 28, last, I have been publishing an "open letter" to Mayor Milam in nearly all the papers in this district, wherein I ask him if he is a prohibitionist or an anti-prohibitionist? Whether he made speeches and voted for Jake Wolters the attorney and leader of the liquor interests of Texas against Morris Sheppard for United States Senator? and whether if elected to Congress he will help Morris Sheppard get us nationwide prohibition ? and Mr. Milam has never answered this open letter yet. I now state to the people whose vote he is seeking that he is an anti-prohibitionist; campaigned and voted against submission ; campaigned and voted for Jake Wolters.
I appeal to the honest bona fide prohibitionists of this district not to be deceived by the cunning tricks of this liquor candidate, Mayor Milam of Fort Worth. We can win prohibition this year if you will be true to your cause, and will settle the question, But you must sound every candidate for Congress or the legislature or senate, and know how he stands, who he runs with, and which side he is on, and who is paying his campaign expenses.
Tarrant county has issued over 21,000 poll tax receipts and certificates this year and the prohibitionists are determined to carry the county this year and will vote their full strength, and I will carry this prohibition vote solid against the liquor mayor of Fort Worth, and beat him two to one among the antis, except the liquor dealers.
In 1912 1 beat two opponents for the legislature-one a pro and the other an anti-got more votes than both of them put together in Tarrant county, and they were both clean, honorable, truthful, popular men, and I never left my desk, and I know ! can do it again against my preesnt opponents.
I have lived 30 years among those people, and we have fought shoulder to shoulder together for Jim Hogg, William J. Bryan and Woodrow Wilson and always won. I have lived in Texas 41 years and have always been a Democrat and never scratched the ticket, not even when Parker run for president.
The first Winchester rifle I ever saw I bought and went to Austin with a band of Confederate veterans to unseat E. J. Davis and seat Richard Coke, as governor of Texas, and I have been fighting for the people ever since. I never asked my people for a political office before this race, and I want them to support me now, for I am going to Congress to do something for that class of men whom Congress has forgotten for fifty years. I know how to do it and will do it. And I am going there too, in spite of the campaign stories. Your friend for the people.
SAM J. HUNTER.

headlines.title: Judge Hunter Proves Pension Certificate Error
headlines.fullText:
Comanche, Texas, May 29, 1914.
To the People of Texas:
Mr. Oscar Callaway, ever since his return from Washington about three or four weeks ago, in his campaign speeches against me, has told his audiences and has published in the newspapers that "Judge Sam J. Hunter of Fort Worth, Texas, is a wealthy, able bodied lawyer and is drawing a pension from the I'nited States government of $24.00 a month." During all this time I suppose he had in his possession a certificate purporting to have been issued by the pension office at Washington certifying said facts. He never published the certificate itself, or showed it to me or any of my friends, so far as I ever heard. I met him on the sidewalk in Comanche Wednesday afternoon and denounced his statement as untrue, when he drew from his pocket a certificate from the Pension office containing the statement that the pension office records showed that: "Sam J. Hunter, of Fort Worth, Texas, was a member of the 167th Illinois Volunteers, in our late Civil War, in Company F. and was now drawing a pension of $24.00 per month." "I read it and told him that it did not refer to me. Believing it to be an honest document, I told him there must be some other Sam Hunter in Fort Worth who was in fact drawing a pension, and phoned my son at Fort Worth to find out and wire me. This was early Thursday morning. Later Thursday my son phoned me that there was no Sam J. Hunter in Fort Worth on the hooks of the Pension Agent of that city, but that he had wired the Commissioner of Pensions at Washington to wire him the facts, On the following morning I received the following original telegram which the Commissioner of Pensions had wired my son :
WASHINGTON, D. C., May 28, 1914.
Ray Hunter.
Fort Worth, Texas,
Only one Sam J. Hunter on pension rolls. His address is Irving, Illinois. No Sam J. Hunter of Fort Worth, Texas, is a United States pensioner.
G. M. Saltzgaber.
Comm'r.
Mayor Milam, as I have been creditably informed, has been whispering around among the people subtantially the same statement, and that I am a Republican. Do the honorable people of this district and state desire such timber to represent them in Congress? I leave the question for you to answer by your Democratic votes on July 25. 1914. PROHIBITIONISTS STAND BY YOUR GUNS AND THE VICTORY IS OURS!
I have written the Commissioner to send me his official certificate confirming the above telegram, and I will publish it when received. Faithfully yours,
SAM J. HUNTER.
